title: Chapter XXXI.

source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Listen also to what I have to say on this other expression which has been adduced, viz., “Christ, who redeemed us from the curse of the law.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: My view of this passage is that Moses, that illustrious servant of God, committed to those who wished to have the right vision, an emblematic law, and also a real law.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Thus, to take an example, after God had made the world, and all things that are in it, in the space of six days, He rested on the seventh day from all His works; by which statement I do not mean to affirm that He rested because He was fatigued, but that He did so as having brought to its perfection every creature which He had resolved to introduce.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And yet in the sequel it, the new law, says: “My Father worketh hitherto, and I work.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Does that mean, then, that He is still making heaven, or sun, or man, or animals, or trees, or any such thing?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Nay; but the meaning is, that when these visible objects were perfectly finished, He rested from that kind of work; while, however, He still continues to work at objects invisible with an inward mode of action, and saves men.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: In like manner, then, the legislator desires also that every individual amongst us should be devoted unceasingly to this kind of work, even as God Himself is; and he enjoins us consequently to rest continuously from secular things, and to engage in no worldly sort of work whatsoever; and this is called our Sabbath.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: This also he added in the law, that nothing senseless should be done but that we should be careful and direct our life in accordance with what is just and righteous.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Now this law was suspended over men, discharging most sharply its curse against those who might transgress it.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But because its subjects, too, were but men, and because, as happens also frequently with us, controversies arose and injuries were inflicted, the law likewise at once, and with the severest equity, made any wrong that was done return upon the head of the wrong-doer; so that, for instance, if a poor man was minded to gather a bundle of wood upon the Sabbath, he was placed under the curse of the law, and exposed to the penalty of instant death.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: The men, therefore, who had been brought up with the Egyptians were thus severely pressed by the restrictive power of the law, and they were unable to bear the penalties and the curses of the law.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But, again, He who is ever the Saviour, our Lord Jesus Christ, came and delivered those men from these pains and curses of the law, forgiving them their offences.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And He indeed did not deal with them as Moses did, putting the severities of the law in force, and granting indulgence to no man for any offence; but He declared that if any man suffered an injury at the hands of his neighbour, he was to forgive him not once only, nor even twice or thrice, nor only seven times, but even unto seventy times seven; but that, on the other hand, if after all this the offender still continued to do such wrong, he ought then, as the last resource, to be brought under the law of Moses, and that no further pardon should be granted to the man who would thus persist in wrong-doing, even after having been forgiven unto seventy times seven.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And He bestowed His forgiveness not only on a transgressor of such a character as that, but even on one who did offence to the Son of man.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But if a man dealt thus with the Holy Spirit, He made him subject to two curses,—namely, to that of the law of Moses, and to that of His own law; to the law of Moses in truth in this present life, but to His own law at the time of the judgment: for His word is this: “It shall not be forgiven him, neither in this world, neither in the world to come.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: There is the law of Moses, thus, that in this world gives pardon to no such person; and there is the law of Christ that punishes in the future world.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: From this, therefore, mark how He confirms the law, not only not destroying it, but fulfilling it.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Thus, then, He redeemed them from that curse of the law which belongs to the present life; and from this fact has come the appellation “the curse of the law.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: This is the whole account which needs be given of that mode of speech.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But, again, why the law is called the “strength of sin,” we shall at once explain in brief to the best of our ability.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Now it is written that “the law is not made for a righteous man, but for the lawless and disobedient, for the ungodly and for sinners.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: In these times, then, before Moses, there was no written law for transgressors; whence also Pharaoh, not knowing the strength of sin, transgressed in the way of afflicting the children of Israel with unrighteous burdens, and despised the Godhead, not only himself, but also all who were with him.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But, not to make any round-about statement, I shall explain the matter briefly as follows.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: There were certain persons of the Egyptian race mingling with the people of Moses, when that people was under his rule in the desert; and when Moses had taken his position on the mount, with the purpose of receiving the law, the impatient people, I do not mean those who were the true Israel, but those who had been intermixed with the Egyptians, set up a calf as their god, in accordance with their ancient custom of worshipping idols, with the notion that by such means they might secure themselves against ever having to pay the proper penalties for their iniquities.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Thus were they altogether ignorant of the strength of their sin.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But when Moses returned (from the mount) and found that out, he issued orders that those men should be put to death with the sword.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: From that occasion a beginning was made in the correct perception of the strength of sin on the part of these persons through the instrumentality of the law of Moses, and for that reason the law has been called the “strength of sin.”
